questions.question: What is the minimum requirement for a `@FunctionalInterface`?
options: Exactly one method of any kind
Exactly one abstract method (can have default/static methods too)
No abstract methods — only default methods
questions.explanation: A functional interface must have exactly one abstract method (SAM — Single Abstract Method). It can have any number of default, static, and private methods.

questions.question: What happens when two interfaces provide the same default method and a class implements both?
options: Compile error — the class must override the method
Both methods are available via different syntax
questions.explanation: The diamond problem: the compiler forces the implementing class to override the conflicting default method and explicitly choose using InterfaceName.super.method().

questions.question: Which feature was added to interfaces in Java 9?
questions.explanation: Java 9 added private methods to interfaces, allowing default methods to share common logic without exposing it in the public API.

questions.question: Which built-in functional interface represents a function that takes a value and returns a boolean?
questions.explanation: Predicate<T> has a test(T) method returning boolean. While Function<T, Boolean> technically works, Predicate is the idiomatic choice and avoids autoboxing.

questions.question: Can an interface have instance fields?
options: No — interface fields are implicitly public static final (constants)
No — interfaces cannot have any fields
questions.explanation: All fields in an interface are implicitly public, static, and final — making them constants. Interfaces cannot hold instance state.
